intent: NEGATIVE FIXTURE: an MLS level-dominance violation (container_t at s0 reading container_file_t at s0:c0 - the target carries a category the source lacks). The MCS mlsconstrain, not a missing TE allow, blocks the read. RuleSteward must DETECT-and-DECLINE.
_kinds_note: BOTH layers agree: authoritative=Constraint (mlsconstrain DENIED), floor=MlsSuspected (scontext level s0 vs tcontext level s0:c0 mismatch). MLS surfaces as the single Constraint bucket; there is no distinct 'Mls' audit2why string (f4 section 8).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow container_t container_file_t:file read;  (BOGUS - mlsconstrain blocks it regardless of the TE allow)
oracle.audit2allow_R: same BOGUS allow container_t container_file_t:file read; plus the constraint comment block
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: N/A - decline scenario. expected.te is rule-free and compiles exit 0.
oracle.audit2why_detail: mlsconstrain file { ioctl read lock execute execute_no_trans } ((h1 dom h2 -Fail-) or (t1 != mcs_constrained_type -Fail-)); Constraint DENIED. Possible cause is the source level (s0) and target level (s0:c0) are different.

triage_expectation.should_decline_with_reason: MLS level-dominance violation: source level s0 does not dominate target level s0:c0 (target has category c0 the source lacks). A TE allow cannot fix an MLS constraint; the fix is an MLS labeling or policy change.
triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl emits allow container_t container_file_t:file read; (audit2allow does this) which compiles, loads, and STILL does not grant access. The floor's MlsSuspected flag (s0 vs s0:c0 level mismatch) catches it offline.
